entry.title: Taming Stale Data: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for LLMs with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees

link.title: Taming Stale Data: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for LLMs with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees

entry.content:
This post studies a recurring question in large-scale LLM reinforcement learning: when a training batch mixes data generated by multiple historical policy versions, can one still write down an explicit monotonic-improvement lower bound for PPO-style updates?

The short answer is yes: under dynamic mixture sampling, the bound can be summarized as “surrogate objective - update-shift penalty - sampling-staleness penalty.”

1. Introduction: Why Should We Care About Off-Policy Training?

When training a large language model with reinforcement learning, the most direct setup is on-policy training: generate a batch of data, update on that batch, then sample again from the updated model.

In large-scale distributed training, though, hundreds of GPUs sample in parallel and model updates take time. By the time a new version is deployed, data generated by older versions are often still sitting in the queue: throwing them away is wasteful, but using them means training on stale data.

That is the central off-policy question: when can data collected by older policies still support an analyzable monotonic-improvement lower bound for a newer one?

We will ultimately see that the lower bound is governed by three pieces: a surrogate objective we try to maximize, an update-shift penalty controlled on the optimization side, and a sampling-staleness penalty controlled on the data side.

In many RLHF / online alignment setups, if we view the prompt as context and the response as action while ignoring long-horizon environment evolution, the problem is often well approximated as a contextual bandit. I still start from the discounted-MDP setting because it lets us write multi-version behavior mixing, sampling staleness, and clipping in one unified language. Section 6 returns to which terms disappear, and which conclusions remain, in the bandit limit.

Related work has already touched neighboring parts of this picture: GePPO studies off-policy sample reuse with policy-improvement guarantees, while Decoupled PPO explicitly separates the behavior policy from the proximal policy. The emphasis here is different: I expand the behavior side into a dynamic mixture of historical policy versions and then split the risk into update increment shift and sampling staleness. You can also read this post as a continuation of the earlier three-policy perspective: here the behavior side is no longer a single policy ⟦INLMATH35MATHEND⟧, but a mixture over historical policies ⟦INLMATH36MATHEND⟧, while ⟦INLMATH37MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH38MATHEND⟧ play the roles of the current reference policy and update target. Even without that earlier post, the only principle needed here is to separate what the current update can control from what comes from behavior-distribution mismatch.

2. Theoretical Foundations

2.1 Basic Setup

We consider a standard Markov Decision Process (MDP) comprising a state space ⟦INLMATH39MATHEND⟧, action space ⟦INLMATH40MATHEND⟧, transition probability ⟦INLMATH41MATHEND⟧, reward function ⟦INLMATH42MATHEND⟧, initial distribution ⟦INLMATH43MATHEND⟧, and discount factor ⟦INLMATH44MATHEND⟧.

The expected cumulative discounted return of policy ⟦INLMATH45MATHEND⟧ is:

⟦DISPMATH9MATHEND⟧

Discounted State Visitation Distribution

We define it as:

⟦DISPMATH10MATHEND⟧

Advantage Function

We define it as:

⟦DISPMATH11MATHEND⟧

Total Variation Distance (TV Distance)

We define it as:

⟦DISPMATH12MATHEND⟧

Throughout, we use ⟦INLMATH46MATHEND⟧ for conditional probability (e.g., ⟦INLMATH47MATHEND⟧) and reserve ⟦INLMATH48MATHEND⟧ for norms.

2.2 Core Tool: Policy Performance Difference Lemma

The starting point of the analysis is the classic performance difference lemma, which writes ⟦INLMATH49MATHEND⟧ exactly as an expectation of old-policy advantage under the new policy’s occupancy measure. This identity goes back to Kakade-Langford style analysis and is also the starting point of TRPO.

Lemma 2.1 (Policy Performance Difference Lemma)

For any policies ⟦INLMATH50MATHEND⟧ (old) and ⟦INLMATH51MATHEND⟧ (new), the performance difference can be expressed as:

⟦DISPMATH1MATHEND⟧

Intuitive understanding: How much better the new policy is than the old equals the “average advantage” obtained by selecting actions according to the new policy under the state distribution visited by the new policy.

3. Performance Improvement Bounds for Single-Policy Sampling

3.1 Distribution Mismatch and Controlling State Shift

The Policy Performance Difference Lemma has a practical issue: the expectation on the right-hand side is computed under ⟦INLMATH52MATHEND⟧ (the new policy’s state distribution), while we can only sample from ⟦INLMATH53MATHEND⟧ (the old policy).

The solution is to decompose the expectation into “expectation under the old distribution + bias term,” then control the bias. The key question is: What is the quantitative relationship between the difference in state distributions and the difference in policies?

Controlling State Distribution Differences

Lemma 3.1 (Relationship Between State Distribution Difference and Policy TV Distance)

⟦DISPMATH2MATHEND⟧

This is an average-divergence / average-TV style bound. It is closer in spirit to CPO / Achiam et al. (2017) than to the classic TRPO presentation based on ⟦INLMATH54MATHEND⟧. I use it here because it lands more naturally on sample averages and is easier to carry into the multi-source setting. Appendix A includes a short proof sketch for how the state-distribution difference is reduced to average TV.

Physical Interpretation

Small differences in policies in action space are “amplified” through environment dynamics into differences in state visitation distributions. The coefficient ⟦INLMATH55MATHEND⟧ reflects the temporal accumulation effect—in long-horizon tasks (⟦INLMATH56MATHEND⟧ close to 1), the amplification is stronger.

Proof Sketch

By deriving the fixed-point equation for discounted visitation distributions and exploiting the ⟦INLMATH57MATHEND⟧ non-expansiveness of stochastic matrices, one can show that state-distribution differences are amplified by policy differences through transition dynamics, yielding the bound in Lemma 3.1. Since the main point of this post is not to re-derive that chain in full, I keep only the idea here and move the proof sketch to Appendix A.

3.2 Policy Performance Improvement Lower Bound

Theorem 3.2 (Policy Performance Improvement Lower Bound)

Define the expected advantage upper bound coefficient ⟦INLMATH58MATHEND⟧. Then:

⟦DISPMATH3MATHEND⟧

where the surrogate objective is:

⟦DISPMATH4MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH59MATHEND⟧ omits the additive constant ⟦INLMATH60MATHEND⟧, so in textbook TRPO notation one could also write the objective as ⟦INLMATH61MATHEND⟧. Also note that ⟦INLMATH62MATHEND⟧ depends on the new policy ⟦INLMATH63MATHEND⟧, so it should be read as a structural coefficient in the bound rather than a tunable hyperparameter.

This lower bound consists of two parts:

  1. Surrogate objective ⟦INLMATH64MATHEND⟧: Can be directly estimated from old policy data via importance sampling; this is the optimization objective of TRPO/PPO.

  2. Policy shift penalty: Increases with the TV distance between new and old policies, explaining why PPO needs to constrain update magnitude.

Core conclusion: this is an explicit improvement lower bound. In particular, when the right-hand side is positive, improvement is guaranteed.

4. Multi-Policy Static Mixture Sampling

4.1 Setup and Unified Modeling (Static Mixture)

In practice, a batch of data may come from multiple policy versions ⟦INLMATH65MATHEND⟧, with respective proportions ⟦INLMATH66MATHEND⟧. How do we extend Theorem 3.2 to this setting?

Core idea: augmented state space

The solution is an elegant modeling technique: treat the policy version index as part of the state.

Define the augmented state space ⟦INLMATH67MATHEND⟧, where ⟦INLMATH68MATHEND⟧ is the policy index set. Under augmented state ⟦INLMATH69MATHEND⟧, the mixture behavior policy is defined as ⟦INLMATH70MATHEND⟧.

The evolution of indices is characterized by the index transition kernel ⟦INLMATH71MATHEND⟧. The augmented MDP inherits the original MDP’s rewards and environment transitions, with indices evolving independently according to ⟦INLMATH72MATHEND⟧.

This technique works because the new policy ⟦INLMATH73MATHEND⟧ has the same return in the augmented MDP as in the original one, so Theorem 3.2 can be applied directly.

4.2 Trajectory-Level Mixture: Simplification and Improvement Bound

The most common scenario is using a single old policy per trajectory: at trajectory start, sample index ⟦INLMATH74MATHEND⟧, and use ⟦INLMATH75MATHEND⟧ throughout. In this case, the index transition kernel is the identity: ⟦INLMATH76MATHEND⟧.

From an engineering perspective, many asynchronous actor-learner systems organize data so that an entire trajectory belongs to a particular policy snapshot, and the learner then mixes trajectories produced by different versions. That setup approximately corresponds to what I call trajectory-level mixture. The word “approximately” matters because different systems need not agree on the exact boundary of a trajectory or sampling unit.

Lemma 4.1 (Structural Simplification for Trajectory-Level Mixture)

(a) The augmented state visitation distribution decomposes as: ⟦INLMATH77MATHEND⟧

(b) The advantage function reduces to: ⟦INLMATH78MATHEND⟧

Intuition for (b): Since the index never changes, all future trajectories starting from augmented state ⟦INLMATH79MATHEND⟧ are generated by the same policy ⟦INLMATH80MATHEND⟧. Therefore, future cumulative returns are entirely determined by ⟦INLMATH81MATHEND⟧, and value functions and advantage functions naturally reduce to their ⟦INLMATH82MATHEND⟧ counterparts.

Consequently, the mixture policy’s return is the weighted average of individual old policies’ returns: ⟦INLMATH83MATHEND⟧.

Improvement bound

Corollary 4.2 (Performance Improvement Lower Bound for Trajectory-Level Mixture)

⟦DISPMATH5MATHEND⟧

This result shows that when mixing trajectories from multiple old policy versions for training, if we construct the loss using importance ratios corresponding to each trajectory’s source policy while controlling the new policy’s deviation from each old policy, the new policy’s performance has a clear improvement lower bound.

Using ⟦INLMATH84MATHEND⟧ is just a compact way to write the result. A slightly tighter version would keep a separate ⟦INLMATH85MATHEND⟧ for each component and then average them with the mixture weights.

5. Dynamic Mixture Sampling and Monotonic Improvement Conditions

5.1 Problem and Unified Modeling (Dynamic Mixture)

Section 4 discussed static mixture—where mixture weights ⟦INLMATH86MATHEND⟧ remain fixed. This section considers the more general dynamic mixture—where sampling gradually transitions to the new policy after it is released.

The previous results characterize improvement of “the new policy relative to the mixture behavior policy.” However, in actual training, what we truly care about is: Does the latest policy ⟦INLMATH87MATHEND⟧ after each update monotonically improve over the previous latest policy ⟦INLMATH88MATHEND⟧?

⟦DISPMATH13MATHEND⟧

Unified Modeling Framework

Two typical forms of dynamic mixture sampling can be uniformly characterized by the index transition kernel ⟦INLMATH89MATHEND⟧:

Trajectory-level mixture (can be viewed as an abstraction of conventional asynchronous training; identity index transition): ⟦INLMATH90MATHEND⟧

Step/segment-level mixture (an abstraction of partial rollout, or segment-based sampling; allows switching): ⟦INLMATH91MATHEND⟧

where ⟦INLMATH92MATHEND⟧ is the switching probability and ⟦INLMATH93MATHEND⟧ is the target index distribution.

5.2 Decomposition and Monotonic Improvement Bound

By introducing the mixture return ⟦INLMATH94MATHEND⟧ as an intermediate bridge, the performance difference decomposes as:

⟦DISPMATH14MATHEND⟧

The first term is handled using Theorem 3.2. The second is the mixture bias term. The way to handle it is to expand ⟦INLMATH95MATHEND⟧ into a weighted sum of ⟦INLMATH96MATHEND⟧ terms, apply a TV-based two-policy lower bound to each component, and then collect everything using ⟦INLMATH97MATHEND⟧. This gives:

⟦DISPMATH15MATHEND⟧

Monotonic Improvement Bound

Combining the above results yields the core theorem:

Theorem 5.1 (Monotonic Improvement Lower Bound Under Dynamic Mixture Sampling)

⟦DISPMATH6MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH98MATHEND⟧ denotes the surrogate objective relative to the behavior policy ⟦INLMATH99MATHEND⟧ (the same shape as ⟦INLMATH100MATHEND⟧ in Section 3, but with the behavior policy generalized from a single ⟦INLMATH101MATHEND⟧ to the mixture ⟦INLMATH102MATHEND⟧).

More explicitly, one can write

⟦DISPMATH16MATHEND⟧

Similarly, define

⟦DISPMATH17MATHEND⟧

This lower bound contains two penalties, so it naturally points to two separate control problems:

  • Update shift penalty: Deviation of the new policy ⟦INLMATH103MATHEND⟧ from the sampling source policy ⟦INLMATH104MATHEND⟧
  • Sampling staleness penalty: Staleness of the sampling source policy ⟦INLMATH105MATHEND⟧ relative to the current policy ⟦INLMATH106MATHEND⟧

5.3 Why Direct Constraints Are Infeasible: Triangle Inequality Decomposition

Before going further, one important qualifier: the infeasibility discussed here concerns an interpretation in which we try to impose the same hard trust-region constraint against every historical source policy. That is not the same as saying PPO-Clip itself explicitly implements such a constraint.

The update-shift penalty in Theorem 5.1 may look controllable through constraints on ⟦INLMATH107MATHEND⟧, but that interpretation quickly runs into a practical infeasibility:

Observation 5.2 (Infeasibility of a Uniform Hard Trust Region)

Suppose the mixture sampling includes two old policies ⟦INLMATH108MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH109MATHEND⟧. If there exists some state ⟦INLMATH110MATHEND⟧ such that ⟦INLMATH111MATHEND⟧, then no policy ⟦INLMATH112MATHEND⟧ can simultaneously satisfy ⟦INLMATH113MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH114MATHEND⟧.

Proof

By the triangle inequality, if both constraints were satisfied, then ⟦INLMATH115MATHEND⟧, a contradiction.

Root Cause

The update shift penalty directly couples ⟦INLMATH116MATHEND⟧ with the historical policy family ⟦INLMATH117MATHEND⟧, whose internal structure is a product of historical training and not controllable by the current update.

Triangle Inequality Decomposition

The solution leverages the triangle inequality of TV distance:

⟦DISPMATH18MATHEND⟧

This decomposes the coupled constraint into two independent parts:

  • Update increment shift ⟦INLMATH118MATHEND⟧: Deviation of the new policy from the current policy, controllable by the optimization side
  • Sampling staleness ⟦INLMATH119MATHEND⟧: Deviation of the current policy from each old policy, must be controlled by the sampling side

Define:

⟦DISPMATH19MATHEND⟧

Corollary 5.3 (Decomposed Monotonic Improvement Lower Bound)

⟦DISPMATH7MATHEND⟧

Why Does Decomposition Solve the Problem?

The key is that after decomposition, ⟦INLMATH120MATHEND⟧ only involves the new policy ⟦INLMATH121MATHEND⟧ and the current policy ⟦INLMATH122MATHEND⟧, completely independent of the structure of the old policy family ⟦INLMATH123MATHEND⟧. So no matter how far apart the old policies are, constraining ⟦INLMATH124MATHEND⟧ remains feasible. This is exactly how the infeasibility in Observation 5.2 is avoided.

Operationally, this leads to a simple separation of concerns:

Control Term Responsible Party Control Mechanism
⟦INLMATH125MATHEND⟧ (update increment shift) Optimization algorithm Policy clipping
⟦INLMATH126MATHEND⟧ (sampling staleness) Sampling system Data filtering, version window

6. Comparison of Trajectory-Level and Step/Segment-Level Mixture

6.1 Mechanism Differences and Estimation Implications

The essential difference between the two mixture mechanisms lies in the structure of the index transition kernel:

  • Trajectory-level mixture: ⟦INLMATH127MATHEND⟧, index never changes
  • Step/segment-level mixture: ⟦INLMATH128MATHEND⟧, allows within-trajectory switching

The correspondence with common engineering terminology is:

  • Trajectory-level mixture here can be roughly understood as an idealized abstraction of “conventional asynchronous training”: data is organized by entire trajectories/episodes belonging to a certain policy version;
  • Step/segment-level mixture here can be roughly understood as an abstraction of “partial rollout”: due to asynchrony between actors and learners, and possible refresh to new policy versions at segment boundaries, using an index transition kernel that allows “within-trajectory version switching” can better approximate this phenomenon. APRIL is a representative systems example of this design pattern, but its main contribution is reducing long-tail rollout bottlenecks rather than providing the monotonic-improvement theory developed here.

The key dividing line is whether Lemma 4.1’s structural simplification holds: trajectory-level mixture satisfies the advantage reduction, while step/segment-level mixture generally does not because future returns depend on the index transition kernel.

Differences in Sampling Staleness ⟦INLMATH129MATHEND⟧

Trajectory-level mixture’s staleness arises from: mixture weights ⟦INLMATH130MATHEND⟧ retaining probability mass on old policies after new policy release.

Step/segment-level mixture has an exponential compression effect in a simplified model: suppose that once the index switches from an old version to the new version it never switches back, and that the switch happens with probability ⟦INLMATH131MATHEND⟧ at each step. Then the marginal mass on the old index under the discounted visitation distribution is

⟦DISPMATH20MATHEND⟧

As long as ⟦INLMATH132MATHEND⟧, the old-policy mass is significantly compressed.

Differences in Surrogate Objective Estimation

Trajectory-level mixture: The advantage function reduces to ⟦INLMATH133MATHEND⟧, with a clear estimation path.

Advantage substitution bias in step/segment-level mixture: If single-policy advantage estimates are used, systematic bias will arise. The reason is that ⟦INLMATH134MATHEND⟧ requires taking expectations over future index switching, while ⟦INLMATH135MATHEND⟧ implicitly assumes “the future always follows ⟦INLMATH136MATHEND⟧.”

Unification Under Bandit Setting

In single-step episode LLM training, with no subsequent state transitions, the estimation problems of both mechanisms unify, with no such bias.

6.2 Risks and Applicable Scenarios

Step/segment-level mixture has another hidden concern: even if single-step importance ratios are clipped, multi-step noise accumulation over long trajectories can still amplify gradient estimation variance. When policy changes per update are large, “behavioral discontinuities” within trajectories may induce heavier-tailed ratio distributions. This is also why Table 6.1 recommends trajectory-level mixture for scenarios with large policy change per update.

Applicable Scenarios

Table 6.1 Applicable Scenarios for Two Mixture Mechanisms

Scenario Characteristics Recommended Mechanism Rationale
Long trajectories, high-frequency updates, strong asynchrony Step/segment-level Can significantly compress ⟦INLMATH137MATHEND⟧
Short trajectories (non-bandit) Trajectory-level ⟦INLMATH138MATHEND⟧ is naturally low
Large policy change per update Trajectory-level Avoids variance amplification
Single-step episode (bandit) Either Choose based on implementation convenience
Need for compromise Segment-level Switch at natural boundaries

Core trade-off: Step/segment-level mixture is stronger on the sampling side (fast staleness removal), while trajectory-level mixture is more stable on the estimation side (easier surrogate objective estimation).

7. Theoretical Foundations of Clipping Mechanisms

7.1 From TV Distance to Sample-Controllable Quantities

Corollary 5.3 tells us that to guarantee monotonic improvement, we need to control the update increment shift ⟦INLMATH139MATHEND⟧. But TV distance is a distribution-level quantity, so how do we control it with samples?

The bridge from theory to samples is the following identity:

Lemma 7.1 (Ratio Difference Representation of TV Distance)

Suppose policy ⟦INLMATH140MATHEND⟧’s support covers the supports of ⟦INLMATH141MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH142MATHEND⟧. Then for any state distribution ⟦INLMATH143MATHEND⟧:

⟦DISPMATH8MATHEND⟧

Note the support-coverage requirement: the behavior policy used in the denominator must assign nonzero mass to the actions that appear in training. For LLMs, this means hard top-k / top-p truncation without smoothing can make some ratios undefined. Section 8 returns to that issue.

Intuitive Understanding

The left side is the TV distance between two distributions (requiring enumeration over all actions), while the right side is the absolute difference of two importance ratios when sampling under ⟦INLMATH144MATHEND⟧. This enables us to estimate and control TV distance using samples.

Sample Representation of ⟦INLMATH145MATHEND⟧

Using Lemma 7.1, setting ⟦INLMATH146MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH147MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH148MATHEND⟧ (the sampling-source policy), we obtain:

⟦DISPMATH21MATHEND⟧

Denoting ⟦INLMATH149MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH150MATHEND⟧, we have:

⟦DISPMATH22MATHEND⟧

This means: If we can ensure ⟦INLMATH151MATHEND⟧ for each sample, we can guarantee ⟦INLMATH152MATHEND⟧.

7.2 Constraining ⟦INLMATH153MATHEND⟧: Two Clipping Options

Method 1: Direct Constraint on Ratio Difference

For each sample ⟦INLMATH154MATHEND⟧, require:

⟦DISPMATH23MATHEND⟧

The clipping interval is ⟦INLMATH155MATHEND⟧, with clipping center at ⟦INLMATH156MATHEND⟧ rather than 1.

Method 2: Constraint on Incremental Ratio

Noting that ⟦INLMATH157MATHEND⟧, we have:

⟦DISPMATH24MATHEND⟧

If we constrain ⟦INLMATH158MATHEND⟧, then

⟦DISPMATH25MATHEND⟧

Taking expectations gives ⟦INLMATH159MATHEND⟧, hence ⟦INLMATH160MATHEND⟧.

This method clips ⟦INLMATH161MATHEND⟧ with center at 1, meaning the clipping constraint itself does not depend on the old policy family ⟦INLMATH162MATHEND⟧. However, if we use the weighted advantage ⟦INLMATH163MATHEND⟧ below, we still need per-sample behavior probabilities (or recorded logprobs) to compute ⟦INLMATH164MATHEND⟧.

Before writing down the clipped objectives, one caveat is worth making explicit: the two inequalities above are hard per-sample constraints at the theory level. The clipped surrogates below are practical approximations meant to keep ⟦INLMATH165MATHEND⟧ in a manageable range; they are not literal guarantees that every optimization step exactly satisfies the hard constraint.

Objective Functions (Three Clipping Mechanisms)

For comparison, we present the complete objective functions for three clipping mechanisms. Suppose the current sample comes from old policy ⟦INLMATH166MATHEND⟧, and denote:

  • ⟦INLMATH167MATHEND⟧ (new policy’s ratio relative to sampling policy)
  • ⟦INLMATH168MATHEND⟧ (current policy’s ratio relative to sampling policy)
  • ⟦INLMATH169MATHEND⟧ (new policy’s incremental ratio relative to current policy)

Note: under trajectory-level mixture (index fixed), ⟦INLMATH170MATHEND⟧, so per-trajectory advantages from the corresponding old policy are consistent; under step/segment-level mixture, replacing ⟦INLMATH171MATHEND⟧ with ⟦INLMATH172MATHEND⟧ introduces advantage-substitution bias (discussed in Section 6), so the advantage/value estimator must reflect future index switching.

Standard PPO (Trajectory-Level Mixture)

Clip ⟦INLMATH173MATHEND⟧ with center at 1

⟦DISPMATH26MATHEND⟧

Clip ⟦INLMATH174MATHEND⟧ with center at ⟦INLMATH175MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH27MATHEND⟧

Clip incremental ratio ⟦INLMATH176MATHEND⟧ with center at 1

where ⟦INLMATH177MATHEND⟧ is the importance-weighted advantage estimate.

7.3 Method Comparison and Selection

Table 7.1 Comparison of Three Clipping Mechanisms

Method Clipped Variable Clipping Center Clipping Interval More Natural Shift Object
Standard PPO ⟦INLMATH178MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH179MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH180MATHEND⟧ New policy relative to ⟦INLMATH181MATHEND⟧
Method 1 ⟦INLMATH182MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH183MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH184MATHEND⟧ New policy relative to ⟦INLMATH185MATHEND⟧
Method 2 ⟦INLMATH186MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH187MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH188MATHEND⟧ New policy relative to ⟦INLMATH189MATHEND⟧

For the hard per-sample constraints discussed earlier, Methods 1 and 2 do directly control ⟦INLMATH190MATHEND⟧. For the clipped surrogates used in practice, however, the more accurate reading is that they exert optimization pressure on different shift objects rather than explicitly imposing a TV constraint.

The Fundamental Problem with Standard PPO Under Multi-Policy Mixture

If we carry over a single-source trust-region intuition, standard PPO’s clipped objective is most naturally read as suppressing further deviation of the new policy from each sampling-source policy ⟦INLMATH191MATHEND⟧. But PPO-Clip does not explicitly impose TV / KL constraints; rather, clipping removes gains from moving farther away from the behavior policy. When the old policies ⟦INLMATH192MATHEND⟧ differ substantially, that optimization pressure is easily dominated by the stalest sources.

Common Advantages of Methods 1 and 2

For the hard-constraint versions introduced earlier, Methods 1 and 2 directly control ⟦INLMATH193MATHEND⟧. For the clipped-surrogate versions used in practice, the more accurate statement is that they redirect optimization pressure from “stay close to every behavior policy at once” toward “control the update around the current policy ⟦INLMATH194MATHEND⟧.” Since ⟦INLMATH195MATHEND⟧ is unique, that target is shared across all sample sources and avoids the structural difficulty behind the infeasibility result.

Method 1 vs Method 2

Comparison Dimension Method 1 (Adaptive Clipping) Method 2 (Incremental Clipping)
Stale samples (⟦INLMATH196MATHEND⟧) Automatically tightens constraints, more conservative May produce large gradient variance
LLM large vocabulary low-probability tokens Allows larger absolute changes (additive) Absolute changes are limited (multiplicative)
Implementation complexity Requires storing ⟦INLMATH197MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH198MATHEND⟧ Needs ⟦INLMATH199MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH200MATHEND⟧ (or stored logprobs) to compute ⟦INLMATH201MATHEND⟧; clipping itself uses only ⟦INLMATH202MATHEND⟧
Advantage function Uses ⟦INLMATH203MATHEND⟧ Uses weighted advantage ⟦INLMATH204MATHEND⟧

Detailed Explanations

(1) Handling Stale Samples

When samples come from very old policies, ⟦INLMATH205MATHEND⟧ can be large.

  • Method 2’s integrand is ⟦INLMATH206MATHEND⟧; even if ⟦INLMATH207MATHEND⟧, the integrand can reach ⟦INLMATH208MATHEND⟧, producing spikes.
  • Method 1 directly constrains ⟦INLMATH209MATHEND⟧; the integrand’s upper bound is always ⟦INLMATH210MATHEND⟧, unaffected by ⟦INLMATH211MATHEND⟧ amplification.

(2) LLM Large Vocabulary Issue

Large language models have many tokens having very small probabilities.

  • Method 2 constrains ⟦INLMATH212MATHEND⟧, which is a multiplicative constraint: if ⟦INLMATH213MATHEND⟧, the allowed absolute change is only ⟦INLMATH214MATHEND⟧.
  • Method 1 constrains ⟦INLMATH215MATHEND⟧, which is an additive constraint: if that token has higher probability under the old policy (e.g., ⟦INLMATH216MATHEND⟧), even if the current probability is very low, faster improvement is allowed - provided the token still has enough observable mass under the sampling policy.

7.4 Staleness Control and Operational Meaning

The discussion so far has focused on optimization-side clipping, but the monotonic-improvement lower bound also contains a sampling-staleness term ⟦INLMATH217MATHEND⟧. This subsection handles the sampling side’s responsibility and then returns to what clipping, as an overall operation, actually means.

Controlling Sampling Staleness

Corollary 5.3 shows that ⟦INLMATH218MATHEND⟧ also enters the monotonic-improvement lower bound, but it cannot be controlled from the optimization side. It has to be handled by the sampling system:

(1) Discarding Stale Data

Set a threshold ⟦INLMATH219MATHEND⟧. For each sample, compute ⟦INLMATH220MATHEND⟧, and discard samples exceeding the threshold.

(2) Controlling Policy Version Window

Limit the number of old policy versions in the mixture sampling, e.g., using only data from the most recent ⟦INLMATH221MATHEND⟧ versions.

Operational Meaning of Clipping

Finally, we clarify the relationship between clipping and the theoretical lower bound.

In Corollary 5.3, the coefficient of ⟦INLMATH222MATHEND⟧, namely ⟦INLMATH223MATHEND⟧, depends on the new policy ⟦INLMATH224MATHEND⟧, so the penalty term cannot simply be replaced by a fixed constant. Operationally, the clipped objective should be read as an approximation to a constrained update, not as a literal theorem with a hand-picked scalar penalty:

Maximize the surrogate objective ⟦INLMATH225MATHEND⟧ subject to the constraint ⟦INLMATH226MATHEND⟧

The clipping objective can be read as a practical approximation to this constrained optimization: clipping keeps the update magnitude in check so that ⟦INLMATH227MATHEND⟧ stays manageable, and then gradient ascent pushes up the surrogate objective within that approximation.

Section Summary

This section established the theoretical foundations of clipping mechanisms:

  1. Lemma 7.1 converts TV distance to sample-level ratio differences, serving as the bridge between theory and implementation
  2. Two constraint methods: the hard-constraint versions of Method 1 (adaptive clipping center) and Method 2 (fixed incremental clipping) both imply ⟦INLMATH228MATHEND⟧; the clipped surrogates used in practice are approximations to this idea
  3. Comparison with standard PPO: under a single-source trust-region intuition, standard PPO applies optimization pressure around the new-vs-behavior shift; Methods 1/2 redirect that pressure to the current policy ⟦INLMATH229MATHEND⟧, which avoids the structural difficulty caused by multiple behavior sources
  4. Method selection: Method 1 (adaptive) is recommended for high staleness or LLM large vocabulary scenarios; Method 2 (incremental) is attractive when you want the clipping center to avoid depending on the old policy family (but data still needs to provide behavior logprobs to compute ⟦INLMATH230MATHEND⟧)
  5. ⟦INLMATH231MATHEND⟧ control is the sampling side’s responsibility, implemented through data filtering and version windows
  6. Clipping is constrained optimization: Maximize the surrogate objective subject to ⟦INLMATH232MATHEND⟧ constraints

8. Handling Training-Inference Inconsistency

8.1 Background and Effective Staleness

In large-scale distributed training, policies on the inference side and training side may be inconsistent:

  • Numerical implementation differences: softmax normalization, quantization, kernel fusion
  • Decoding rule differences: temperature scaling, top-p/top-k sampling

Let the behavior policy modeled on the training side be ⟦INLMATH233MATHEND⟧, while the policy actually sampling on the inference side is ⟦INLMATH234MATHEND⟧.

The mismatch discussed here is between the behavior policy and the current training policy. It is distinct from the policy-vs-reference-model KL regularization that is common in RLHF; that is a different regularization axis.

Effective Staleness

Define effective staleness:

⟦DISPMATH29MATHEND⟧

This definition simultaneously covers version staleness and training-inference implementation differences.

8.2 Actionable Control

By Lemma 7.1, ⟦INLMATH235MATHEND⟧ can be written in a sample-computable form. Given threshold ⟦INLMATH236MATHEND⟧, if training only uses samples satisfying ⟦INLMATH237MATHEND⟧, then the effective staleness on the conditional distribution of retained samples (which we may denote by ⟦INLMATH238MATHEND⟧) can be controlled to at most ⟦INLMATH239MATHEND⟧. This controls the filtered training distribution, not the original sampling distribution’s ⟦INLMATH240MATHEND⟧.

Key Implementation Points

  1. Behavior denominator alignment: The behavior probability in the loss should use the inference-side recorded ⟦INLMATH241MATHEND⟧
  2. Probability smoothing: If the inference side has truncation (e.g., top-k), ensure ratios are valid and the support-coverage condition required by Lemma 7.1 still holds

9. Practical Guidelines

Core Theoretical Framework

The structure of the monotonic improvement lower bound is:

⟦DISPMATH30MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH242MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH243MATHEND⟧ are just compressed notation for the theoretical coefficients introduced earlier. Concretely, one can read them as

⟦DISPMATH31MATHEND⟧

up to the exact naming convention used in the intermediate statements. They are not free hyperparameters.

Separation of Concerns Principle

Control Term Responsible Party Control Mechanism Specific Operation
⟦INLMATH244MATHEND⟧ Optimization algorithm Policy clipping Clip update increments (e.g., clip ⟦INLMATH245MATHEND⟧)
⟦INLMATH246MATHEND⟧ Sampling system Data filtering Discard stale samples
⟦INLMATH247MATHEND⟧ Sampling system Version window Use only most recent ⟦INLMATH248MATHEND⟧ versions

Clipping Method Selection

Scenario Recommended Method Rationale
High staleness Method 1 (adaptive) Automatically tightens constraints for stale samples
Implementation simplicity prioritized Method 2 (incremental) Clipping form is simpler, though behavior logprobs are still needed for ⟦INLMATH249MATHEND⟧
LLM large vocabulary Method 1 Avoids slow updates for low-probability tokens

Handling Training-Inference Inconsistency

  • Use inference-side recorded ⟦INLMATH250MATHEND⟧ as the behavior denominator
  • Compress effective staleness through sample filtering

Appendix

A. Proof Sketch: From State-Distribution Difference to Average TV

A standard starting point for Lemma 3.1 is the fixed-point equation of discounted state visitation distributions:

⟦DISPMATH32MATHEND⟧

Subtracting the two equations and rearranging expresses ⟦INLMATH251MATHEND⟧ in terms of the difference between policy-induced transition kernels acting on the old distribution. Taking an ⟦INLMATH252MATHEND⟧ upper bound, using the non-expansiveness of Markov kernels in ⟦INLMATH253MATHEND⟧, and then applying

⟦DISPMATH33MATHEND⟧

one obtains the average-TV control

⟦DISPMATH34MATHEND⟧

I omit the linear-operator algebra and constant bookkeeping here because the post only uses the final average-TV form.

B. Quick Reference for Key Symbols

Symbol Meaning
⟦INLMATH254MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH255MATHEND⟧ Latest policy at round ⟦INLMATH256MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH257MATHEND⟧-th old policy
⟦INLMATH258MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH259MATHEND⟧ Discounted state visitation distribution, advantage function
⟦INLMATH260MATHEND⟧ TV distance between two policies at state ⟦INLMATH261MATHEND⟧
⟦INLMATH262MATHEND⟧ Mixture behavior policy at round ⟦INLMATH263MATHEND⟧
⟦INLMATH264MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH265MATHEND⟧ Index transition kernel, initial index distribution
⟦INLMATH266MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH267MATHEND⟧ Update increment shift, sampling staleness
⟦INLMATH268MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH269MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH270MATHEND⟧ Clipping radius, staleness threshold, version window
⟦INLMATH271MATHEND⟧ Expected advantage upper bound coefficient

References

  1. John Schulman, Sergey Levine, Philipp Moritz, Michael I. Jordan, Pieter Abbeel. “Trust Region Policy Optimization” (TRPO). arXiv:1502.05477. https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.05477
  2. Joshua Achiam, David Held, Aviv Tamar, Pieter Abbeel. “Constrained Policy Optimization” (CPO). arXiv:1705.10528. https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.10528
  3. John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, Oleg Klimov. “Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms” (PPO). arXiv:1707.06347. https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347
  4. James Queeney, Ioannis Ch. Paschalidis, Christos G. Cassandras. “Generalized Proximal Policy Optimization with Sample Reuse” (GePPO). arXiv:2111.00072. https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00072
  5. Yuzhen Zhou, Jiajun Li, Yusheng Su, et al. “APRIL: Active Partial Rollouts in Reinforcement Learning to Tame Long-tail Generation” (APRIL; partial rollout). arXiv:2509.18521. https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.18521

  6. Jacob Hilton, Karl Cobbe, John Schulman. “Batch size-invariance for policy optimization” (Decoupled PPO). arXiv:2110.00641. https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.00641

  7. Sham Kakade, John Langford. “Approximately Optimal Approximate Reinforcement Learning”. ICML 2002. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.5555/645531.657706
@misc{WangZhang2025OffPolicyLLMRL,
	author       = {Wang, Xihuai and Zhang, Shao},
	title        = {Taming Stale Data: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for LLMs with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees},
	year         = {2025},
	month        = dec,
	day          = {17},
	url          = {https://xihuai18.github.io/reinforcement-learning/2025/12/17/offpolicy-en.html},
	urldate      = {2025-12-17}
}
entry.summary: This post derives an off-policy view of LLM reinforcement learning: from single-policy performance bounds to multi-policy mixture sampling, with monotonic-improvement conditions, a decomposition via triangle inequality into update shift and sampling staleness, and practical clipping and filtering strategies.

entry.title: 驯服陈旧数据：LLM 强化学习的异策略训练与单调提升条件

link.title: 驯服陈旧数据：LLM 强化学习的异策略训练与单调提升条件

entry.content:
本文研究一个在大规模 LLM 强化学习里反复出现的问题：当一个训练 batch 同时混入多个历史策略版本生成的数据时，能否仍为 PPO 式更新写出显式的单调改进下界？

先说结论：在动态混合采样下，这个下界可以概括为”代理目标 - 更新偏移惩罚 - 采样陈旧性惩罚”。

1. 引言：为什么要关心”异策略”训练？

用强化学习训练大语言模型，最直接的做法是同策略（on-policy）训练：模型生成一批数据，立刻用这批数据更新，再用更新后的模型采样下一批。

但在大规模分布式训练里，数百个 GPU 并行采样，模型更新本身也有延迟。新版本发布时，旧版本生成的数据往往还留在队列里：直接丢掉太浪费，继续使用又担心数据已经过时。

这就是异策略（off-policy）训练面对的核心问题：用旧策略采集的数据来更新新策略，什么条件仍足以保证一个可分析的单调提升下界？

最终会看到，这个下界由三部分共同决定：一个可最大化的代理目标、一个由优化侧裁剪控制的更新偏移惩罚、以及一个由采样侧过滤控制的陈旧性惩罚。

在不少 RLHF / online alignment 设定里，若把 prompt 看作 context、response 看作 action，并忽略长程环境状态演化，问题常被近似为 contextual bandit。本文仍先在一般折扣 MDP 上推导，是为了把”多版本行为策略混合、采样陈旧性、裁剪机制”这些结构一次性写清楚；到第六部分再看 bandit 化之后哪些地方会明显简化、哪些结论会保留。

相关工作里，GePPO 讨论了样本复用下的 off-policy policy improvement guarantee，Decoupled PPO 则把 behavior policy 与 proximal policy 显式区分开。本文的着眼点不同：这里把行为侧进一步展开成多个历史策略版本的动态混合，并把风险拆成”更新增量偏移”与”采样陈旧性”两项。

也可以把这篇文章看成前一篇”三策略视角”文章的延伸：这里把行为侧从单一 ⟦INLMATH34MATHEND⟧ 显式展开成历史策略混合 ⟦INLMATH35MATHEND⟧，⟦INLMATH36MATHEND⟧ 与 ⟦INLMATH37MATHEND⟧ 分别扮演当前参考策略与更新目标。即使没读过上一篇，也只需记住这里的核心思路：把当前更新可控的部分，和行为分布失配造成的部分分开分析。

2. 理论基础

2.1 基本设定

考虑一个标准的马尔可夫决策过程（MDP），包括状态空间 ⟦INLMATH38MATHEND⟧、动作空间 ⟦INLMATH39MATHEND⟧、转移概率 ⟦INLMATH40MATHEND⟧、奖励函数 ⟦INLMATH41MATHEND⟧、初始状态分布 ⟦INLMATH42MATHEND⟧ 和折扣因子 ⟦INLMATH43MATHEND⟧。

策略 ⟦INLMATH44MATHEND⟧ 的期望累计折扣回报：

⟦DISPMATH9MATHEND⟧

折扣状态访问分布

⟦DISPMATH10MATHEND⟧

优势函数

⟦DISPMATH11MATHEND⟧

全变差距离（TV 距离）

⟦DISPMATH12MATHEND⟧

本文统一用 ⟦INLMATH45MATHEND⟧ 表示条件概率（例如 ⟦INLMATH46MATHEND⟧），⟦INLMATH47MATHEND⟧ 保留给范数。

2.2 核心工具：性能差分引理

全文的起点是经典的 performance difference lemma：把 ⟦INLMATH48MATHEND⟧ 精确写成新策略占据分布下对旧策略优势的期望。这一恒等式可追溯到 Kakade-Langford 的分析，也是 TRPO 推导的起点。

引理 2.1（性能差分引理）

对任意旧策略 ⟦INLMATH49MATHEND⟧ 和新策略 ⟦INLMATH50MATHEND⟧，性能差异可写成：

⟦DISPMATH1MATHEND⟧

直观理解：新策略带来的改进，等于它自身访问到的状态分布下，按它选动作所得到的平均优势。

3. 单策略采样的性能改进下界

3.1 分布不匹配与分布差异控制

性能差分引理有一个实际难题：右侧的期望是在新策略的状态分布 ⟦INLMATH51MATHEND⟧ 下计算的，而我们只能从旧策略的分布 ⟦INLMATH52MATHEND⟧ 中采样。

思路是：把期望分解成”旧分布下的期望”与”偏差项”两部分，再对偏差项加以控制。关键问题是：状态分布的差异与策略的差异之间有怎样的定量关系？

状态分布差异的控制

引理 3.1（状态分布差异与策略 TV 距离的关系）

⟦DISPMATH2MATHEND⟧

这里采用的是 average-divergence / average-TV 风格的写法。它不是 TRPO 里更常见的 ⟦INLMATH53MATHEND⟧ 版本，而更接近 CPO / Achiam et al. (2017) 那类”用平均散度刻画性能差距”的表述；这样写更容易落到样本平均，也更方便后文处理多源采样与陈旧性。关于从 ⟦INLMATH54MATHEND⟧ 到 average TV 的 proof sketch 见附录 A。

物理意义

策略在动作空间上的微小差异，会被环境动力学”放大”成状态访问分布的差异。上式中的系数 ⟦INLMATH55MATHEND⟧ 反映了时间累积效应——在长时域任务里（⟦INLMATH56MATHEND⟧ 接近 1），放大效应更明显。

证明思路

证明通常从折扣访问分布的不动点方程出发，再配合 average-divergence 风格的性能界完成。本文的重点不在复现这条证明链，而在于如何把它推广到多源采样与陈旧数据场景，所以正文只保留思路，附录 A 给出一个简短的 proof sketch。

3.2 策略性能改进下界

定理 3.2（策略性能改进下界）

定义期望优势上界系数 ⟦INLMATH57MATHEND⟧，则：

⟦DISPMATH3MATHEND⟧

其中代理目标为：

⟦DISPMATH4MATHEND⟧

这里的 ⟦INLMATH58MATHEND⟧ 省略了与 ⟦INLMATH59MATHEND⟧ 无关的常数项 ⟦INLMATH60MATHEND⟧；若写成更接近 TRPO 教科书的形式，就是 ⟦INLMATH61MATHEND⟧。另外，⟦INLMATH62MATHEND⟧ 本身依赖新策略 ⟦INLMATH63MATHEND⟧，所以它更像一个出现在下界表达式中的结构性系数，不宜在实践里直接当作固定超参数。

这个下界由两部分组成：

  1. 代理目标 ⟦INLMATH64MATHEND⟧：可通过旧策略数据用重要性采样直接估计，它是 TRPO 的经典 surrogate，也是 PPO clipped / penalized 目标的出发点。

  2. 策略偏移惩罚：随着新旧策略 TV 距离的增大而增加，这解释了为何 PPO 等算法需要限制更新幅度。

核心结论：这条定理给出了一个显式的改进下界；当右侧为正时，就能保证性能改进。

4. 多策略静态混合采样

4.1 问题设定与统一建模（静态混合）

实际训练中，一个批次的数据可能来自多个策略版本 ⟦INLMATH65MATHEND⟧，各版本占比分别为 ⟦INLMATH66MATHEND⟧。如何把定理 3.2 推广到这种情形？

核心思想：扩展状态空间

做法很直接：把策略版本索引并入状态空间。

定义扩展状态空间 ⟦INLMATH67MATHEND⟧，其中 ⟦INLMATH68MATHEND⟧ 是策略索引集合。在扩展状态 ⟦INLMATH69MATHEND⟧ 下，混合行为策略定义为 ⟦INLMATH70MATHEND⟧。

索引的演化由索引转移核 ⟦INLMATH71MATHEND⟧ 刻画。扩展 MDP 继承原始 MDP 的奖励和环境转移，索引按 ⟦INLMATH72MATHEND⟧ 独立演化。

这个技巧之所以有效，是因为新策略 ⟦INLMATH73MATHEND⟧ 在扩展 MDP 上的回报与原始 MDP 中的回报相同，因此可直接套用定理 3.2。

4.2 轨迹级混合：结构简化与改进下界

最常见的情形是每条轨迹只使用一个旧策略：在轨迹开始时采样索引 ⟦INLMATH74MATHEND⟧，整条轨迹都用策略 ⟦INLMATH75MATHEND⟧。此时索引转移核为恒等转移：⟦INLMATH76MATHEND⟧。

工程上，许多异步 actor-learner 架构中，采样端若按”整条轨迹归属于某个策略快照”的方式组织数据，learner 再混合使用不同版本的整条轨迹更新，这就大致对应这里的轨迹级混合。之所以说”大致”，是因为不同系统对”轨迹 / 采样单元”的切分边界未必完全一致。

引理 4.1（轨迹级混合的结构简化）

(a) 扩展状态访问分布分解为：⟦INLMATH77MATHEND⟧

(b) 优势函数还原为：⟦INLMATH78MATHEND⟧

(b) 的直观理解：由于索引永不变，从扩展状态 ⟦INLMATH79MATHEND⟧ 出发的所有未来轨迹都由同一个策略 ⟦INLMATH80MATHEND⟧ 生成。因此未来的累计回报完全由 ⟦INLMATH81MATHEND⟧ 决定，价值函数和优势函数自然还原为 ⟦INLMATH82MATHEND⟧ 的对应量。

混合策略的回报也就是各旧策略回报的加权平均：⟦INLMATH83MATHEND⟧。

改进下界

推论 4.2（轨迹级混合的性能改进下界）

⟦DISPMATH5MATHEND⟧

这里取 ⟦INLMATH84MATHEND⟧ 只是为了写得更紧凑；更细致的写法是让每个分量各自带自己的 ⟦INLMATH85MATHEND⟧ 再加权求和。也就是说，这里牺牲了一点紧度，换来更统一的展示形式。

结论是：只要对每条轨迹用对应旧策略的重要性比率构造损失，并控制新策略与各旧策略的偏移，混合训练就仍有明确的改进下界。

5. 动态混合采样与单调提升条件

5.1 问题与统一建模（动态混合）

上一节讨论的是静态混合——混合权重 ⟦INLMATH86MATHEND⟧ 固定不变。本节考虑更一般的动态混合——新策略发布后，采样逐步由新策略接管的过程。

前面的结论刻画了”新策略相对于混合行为策略”的改进。但实际训练中真正关心的是：每轮更新后的最新策略 ⟦INLMATH87MATHEND⟧ 相对于上一轮的 ⟦INLMATH88MATHEND⟧ 是否单调提升？

⟦DISPMATH13MATHEND⟧

统一建模框架

动态混合采样的两种典型形式都可以用索引转移核 ⟦INLMATH89MATHEND⟧ 统一刻画：

轨迹级混合（对应常规异步训练的一种抽象；索引恒等转移）：⟦INLMATH90MATHEND⟧

步/段级混合（partial rollout，也可理解为段式采样的一种抽象；允许切换）：⟦INLMATH91MATHEND⟧

其中 ⟦INLMATH92MATHEND⟧ 是切换概率，⟦INLMATH93MATHEND⟧ 是目标索引分布。

5.2 分解与单调提升下界

引入混合回报 ⟦INLMATH94MATHEND⟧ 作为中间桥梁，性能差异可以分解为：

⟦DISPMATH14MATHEND⟧

第一项可用定理 3.2 处理。第二项是混合偏差项：先把 ⟦INLMATH95MATHEND⟧ 写成各个 ⟦INLMATH96MATHEND⟧ 的加权和，再对每一项套用基于 TV 距离的两策略下界，最后用 ⟦INLMATH97MATHEND⟧ 统一收口，得到：

⟦DISPMATH15MATHEND⟧

单调提升下界

合并以上结果，得到核心定理：

定理 5.1（动态混合采样下的单调提升下界）

⟦DISPMATH6MATHEND⟧

其中 ⟦INLMATH98MATHEND⟧ 表示”相对行为策略 ⟦INLMATH99MATHEND⟧ 的代理目标”（与第三部分的 ⟦INLMATH100MATHEND⟧ 同形，只是把行为策略从单一 ⟦INLMATH101MATHEND⟧ 推广到混合 ⟦INLMATH102MATHEND⟧）。

更具体地，可写为

⟦DISPMATH16MATHEND⟧

类似地，记

⟦DISPMATH17MATHEND⟧

下界里有两个惩罚项，对应双重控制：

  • 更新偏移惩罚：新策略 ⟦INLMATH103MATHEND⟧ 相对于采样来源策略 ⟦INLMATH104MATHEND⟧ 的偏移；
  • 采样陈旧性惩罚：采样来源策略 ⟦INLMATH105MATHEND⟧ 相对于当前策略 ⟦INLMATH106MATHEND⟧ 的陈旧性。

5.3 直接约束为何不可行：三角不等式分解

先加一句限定：下面讨论的不可行性针对的是”试图对每个历史来源都施加统一硬 trust-region 约束”这种解释，并不等于说 PPO-Clip 本身显式实现了这样的约束。

定理 5.1 中的更新偏移惩罚项看起来可以通过约束 ⟦INLMATH107MATHEND⟧ 控制；但若把它理解成上述统一硬约束，就会撞上一个实际的不可行性问题：

观察 5.2（统一硬 trust-region 的不可行性）

设混合采样包含两个旧策略 ⟦INLMATH108MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH109MATHEND⟧，若存在某个状态 ⟦INLMATH110MATHEND⟧ 使得 ⟦INLMATH111MATHEND⟧，则不存在任何策略 ⟦INLMATH112MATHEND⟧ 能同时满足 ⟦INLMATH113MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH114MATHEND⟧。

证明

由三角不等式，若同时满足这两个约束，则 ⟦INLMATH115MATHEND⟧，矛盾。

问题根源

更新偏移惩罚项把 ⟦INLMATH116MATHEND⟧ 与历史策略族 ⟦INLMATH117MATHEND⟧ 直接耦合，而后者的内部结构是历史训练的产物，不受当前更新控制。

三角不等式分解

解法是利用 TV 距离的三角不等式：

⟦DISPMATH18MATHEND⟧

把耦合约束拆成两个独立部分：

  • 更新增量偏移 ⟦INLMATH118MATHEND⟧：新策略相对于当前策略的偏离，由优化侧控制；
  • 采样陈旧性 ⟦INLMATH119MATHEND⟧：当前策略相对于各旧策略的偏离，由采样侧控制。

定义：

⟦DISPMATH19MATHEND⟧

推论 5.3（分解后的单调提升下界）

⟦DISPMATH7MATHEND⟧

为什么分解能解决问题？

关键在于：分解后的 ⟦INLMATH120MATHEND⟧ 只涉及新策略 ⟦INLMATH121MATHEND⟧ 和当前策略 ⟦INLMATH122MATHEND⟧，与旧策略族 ⟦INLMATH123MATHEND⟧ 的结构完全无关。因此无论旧策略之间差异多大，约束 ⟦INLMATH124MATHEND⟧ 都是可行的——这正是观察 5.2 不可行性问题的解法。

对应的工程原则就是职责分离：

控制项 负责方 控制手段
⟦INLMATH125MATHEND⟧（更新增量偏移） 优化算法 策略裁剪
⟦INLMATH126MATHEND⟧（采样陈旧性） 采样系统 数据过滤、版本窗口

6. 轨迹级与步/段级混合的比较

6.1 机制差异与估计影响

两类混合机制的本质区别在于索引转移核的结构：

  • 轨迹级混合：⟦INLMATH127MATHEND⟧，索引永不改变；
  • 步/段级混合：⟦INLMATH128MATHEND⟧，允许轨迹内切换。

与常见工程术语的对应关系：

  • 轨迹级混合可大致理解为常规异步训练的理想化抽象：数据按整条轨迹 / episode 归属于某个策略版本；
  • 步/段级混合可大致理解为 partial rollout（段式采样） 的抽象：由于 actor 与 learner 异步，段边界处可能刷新到新策略版本，用索引转移核允许”轨迹内部版本切换”，可以更贴切地刻画这种现象。APRIL 提供了这类系统设计的一个代表性例子，但它的主要贡献是缓解长尾 rollout 的系统瓶颈，而不是给出本文所需的单调改进理论。

关键分水岭在于引理 4.1 的结构简化是否成立：轨迹级混合满足优势函数还原；步/段级混合一般不满足，因为未来回报会受索引转移核影响。

采样陈旧性 ⟦INLMATH129MATHEND⟧ 的差异

轨迹级混合的陈旧性来源于：混合权重 ⟦INLMATH130MATHEND⟧ 在新策略发布后仍对旧策略保留一定比例。

步/段级混合在一个简化模型下具有指数压缩效应：设索引一旦从旧版本切到新版本就不再切回，每一步以概率 ⟦INLMATH131MATHEND⟧ 完成切换，则折扣访问分布下旧索引的边缘质量为

⟦DISPMATH20MATHEND⟧

只要 ⟦INLMATH132MATHEND⟧，旧策略的权重就会被明显压缩。

代理目标估计的差异

轨迹级混合：优势函数还原为 ⟦INLMATH133MATHEND⟧，估计路径清晰。

步/段级混合的优势替代偏差：若沿用单策略优势估计，会产生系统性偏差。原因是 ⟦INLMATH134MATHEND⟧ 需要对未来索引切换取期望，而 ⟦INLMATH135MATHEND⟧ 隐含了”未来始终沿用 ⟦INLMATH136MATHEND⟧”的假设。

Bandit 设定下的统一

在单步 episode 的 LLM 训练中没有后续状态转移，两类机制的估计问题就此统一，不存在上述偏差。

6.2 风险与适用场景

步/段级混合还有一个隐患：即使单步重要性比值被裁剪，长轨迹下多步噪声叠加仍会放大梯度估计方差。每次更新的策略变化幅度较大时，轨迹内部的”行为突变”可能引出更重尾的比值分布。这也是表 6.1 中”每次更新策略变化幅度大”场景推荐轨迹级混合的原因。

适用场景

表 6.1　两类混合机制的适用场景

场景特征 推荐机制 理由
长轨迹、高频更新、强异步 步/段级 可显著压缩 ⟦INLMATH137MATHEND⟧
短轨迹（非Bandit） 轨迹级 ⟦INLMATH138MATHEND⟧ 自然较低
每次更新策略变化幅度大 轨迹级 避免方差放大
单步episode（Bandit） 均可 按实现便利选择
需要折中方案 段级 在自然边界切换

核心权衡：步/段级混合在采样侧更强（快速去陈旧），轨迹级混合在估计侧更稳（代理目标更容易估计）。

7. 裁剪机制的理论基础

7.1 从 TV 距离到样本可控量

推论 5.3 告诉我们，要保证单调提升，需要控制更新增量偏移 ⟦INLMATH139MATHEND⟧。但 TV 距离是分布层面的量，如何用样本来控制？

连接理论和样本的是下面这个恒等式：

引理 7.1（TV 距离的比值差表示）

设策略 ⟦INLMATH140MATHEND⟧ 的支撑覆盖 ⟦INLMATH141MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH142MATHEND⟧ 的支撑，则对任意状态分布 ⟦INLMATH143MATHEND⟧：

⟦DISPMATH8MATHEND⟧

注意：这里默认作为分母的行为策略在参与训练的动作上具有支撑覆盖。对 LLM 来说，这意味着若推理端使用带硬截断的 top-k / top-p 采样而不做平滑，一些比值可能根本无定义；第 8 节会回到这个问题。

直观理解

左侧是两个分布之间的 TV 距离（要遍历所有动作），右侧是在 ⟦INLMATH144MATHEND⟧ 下采样时两个重要性比值之差的绝对值。这样就能用样本估计和控制 TV 距离。

⟦INLMATH145MATHEND⟧ 的样本表示

利用引理 7.1，取 ⟦INLMATH146MATHEND⟧，⟦INLMATH147MATHEND⟧，⟦INLMATH148MATHEND⟧（采样来源策略），可得：

⟦DISPMATH21MATHEND⟧

记 ⟦INLMATH149MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH150MATHEND⟧，则：

⟦DISPMATH22MATHEND⟧

这意味着：若在理论上硬性要求每个样本都满足 ⟦INLMATH151MATHEND⟧，就能保证 ⟦INLMATH152MATHEND⟧。

7.2 约束 ⟦INLMATH153MATHEND⟧：两种裁剪方式

方法一：直接约束比值差

对每个样本 ⟦INLMATH154MATHEND⟧ 要求：

⟦DISPMATH23MATHEND⟧

即裁剪区间为 ⟦INLMATH155MATHEND⟧，裁剪中心是 ⟦INLMATH156MATHEND⟧ 而不是 1。

方法二：约束增量比值

注意到 ⟦INLMATH157MATHEND⟧，所以

⟦DISPMATH24MATHEND⟧

如果进一步在理论上硬性约束 ⟦INLMATH158MATHEND⟧，由于 ⟦INLMATH159MATHEND⟧，可以证明 ⟦INLMATH160MATHEND⟧。

这种方法直接对 ⟦INLMATH161MATHEND⟧ 以 1 为中心裁剪，裁剪约束本身不依赖旧策略 ⟦INLMATH162MATHEND⟧。但若采用后文的 ⟦INLMATH163MATHEND⟧，仍需要每条样本的行为概率 ⟦INLMATH164MATHEND⟧（或记录的 logprob）来计算 ⟦INLMATH165MATHEND⟧。

先强调一点：上面两条都是理论上的逐样本硬约束。下面写出的 clipped surrogate 只是实践中的近似实现，目的是把 ⟦INLMATH166MATHEND⟧ 控制在可接受的范围内，而不是让每一步优化都自动满足严格保证。设当前样本来自旧策略 ⟦INLMATH167MATHEND⟧，记：

  • ⟦INLMATH168MATHEND⟧（新策略相对采样策略的比值）；
  • ⟦INLMATH169MATHEND⟧（当前策略相对采样策略的比值）；
  • ⟦INLMATH170MATHEND⟧（新策略相对当前策略的增量比值）。

说明：若是轨迹级混合（索引不变），则 ⟦INLMATH171MATHEND⟧，可直接用每条轨迹对应旧策略的优势估计；若是步/段级混合，直接用 ⟦INLMATH172MATHEND⟧ 代替 ⟦INLMATH173MATHEND⟧ 会引入优势替代偏差（第六部分详述），需要用能反映未来索引切换的优势 / 价值估计。

标准 PPO（轨迹级混合时）

以 1 为中心裁剪 ⟦INLMATH174MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH25MATHEND⟧

方法一

以 ⟦INLMATH175MATHEND⟧ 为中心裁剪 ⟦INLMATH176MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH26MATHEND⟧

方法二

以 1 为中心裁剪增量比值 ⟦INLMATH177MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH27MATHEND⟧

其中 ⟦INLMATH178MATHEND⟧ 是经过重要性加权的优势估计。

7.3 方法对比与选型

表 7.1　三种裁剪机制的对比

方法 裁剪变量 裁剪中心 裁剪区间 更自然对应的偏移对象
标准PPO ⟦INLMATH179MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH180MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH181MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH182MATHEND⟧ 相对 ⟦INLMATH183MATHEND⟧ 的偏移
方法一 ⟦INLMATH184MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH185MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH186MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH187MATHEND⟧ 相对 ⟦INLMATH188MATHEND⟧ 的偏移
方法二 ⟦INLMATH189MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH190MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH191MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH192MATHEND⟧ 相对 ⟦INLMATH193MATHEND⟧ 的偏移

对前文的逐样本硬约束版本而言，方法一 / 二确实直接控制 ⟦INLMATH194MATHEND⟧；而这里的 clipped surrogate 对应的是更温和的说法：它们分别对不同的偏移对象施加主要的优化压力。

标准 PPO 的根本问题（多策略混合）

沿用单源 PPO 的 trust-region 直觉，标准 PPO 的 clip 目标最自然的解读是：它试图抑制新策略相对每个采样来源策略 ⟦INLMATH195MATHEND⟧ 的继续偏离。但 PPO-Clip 本身并不显式施加 TV / KL 约束，而是通过裁剪移除”继续远离行为策略”的收益；当各旧策略 ⟦INLMATH196MATHEND⟧ 之间差异显著时，这种优化压力容易被最陈旧的策略所牵制。

方法一与方法二的共同优势

对前文的逐样本硬约束版本而言，方法一和方法二直接控制的是 ⟦INLMATH197MATHEND⟧。落到 clipped surrogate 的实践版本时，更准确的说法是：它们把主要优化压力从”同时贴近每个采样来源策略”改成”围绕当前策略 ⟦INLMATH198MATHEND⟧ 控制更新增量”。由于 ⟦INLMATH199MATHEND⟧ 是唯一确定的，这个目标对所有来源样本都一致，就绕开了统一硬 trust-region 约束的结构性困难。

方法一 vs 方法二

比较维度 方法一（自适应裁剪） 方法二（增量裁剪）
陈旧样本（⟦INLMATH200MATHEND⟧） 自动收紧约束，更保守 可能产生大梯度方差
LLM大词表低概率token 允许较大绝对变化（加法型） 绝对变化受限（乘法型）
实现复杂度 需存储 ⟦INLMATH201MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH202MATHEND⟧ 需 ⟦INLMATH203MATHEND⟧ 与 ⟦INLMATH204MATHEND⟧（或存储的 logprob）以计算 ⟦INLMATH205MATHEND⟧；裁剪本身仅用 ⟦INLMATH206MATHEND⟧
优势函数 使用 ⟦INLMATH207MATHEND⟧ 使用加权优势 ⟦INLMATH208MATHEND⟧
维度一：陈旧样本处理

样本来自很旧的策略时，⟦INLMATH209MATHEND⟧ 可能很大。

  • 方法二的被积函数为 ⟦INLMATH210MATHEND⟧，即便 ⟦INLMATH211MATHEND⟧，被积函数仍可能达到 ⟦INLMATH212MATHEND⟧，产生尖峰。
  • 方法一直接约束 ⟦INLMATH213MATHEND⟧，被积函数上界恒为 ⟦INLMATH214MATHEND⟧，不会被 ⟦INLMATH215MATHEND⟧ 放大。
维度二：LLM 大词表问题

大语言模型词表规模巨大，大量 token 的概率极小。

  • 方法二约束 ⟦INLMATH216MATHEND⟧，这是乘法型约束：若 ⟦INLMATH217MATHEND⟧，允许的绝对变化仅 ⟦INLMATH218MATHEND⟧。
  • 方法一约束 ⟦INLMATH219MATHEND⟧，尺度由采样策略概率 ⟦INLMATH220MATHEND⟧ 决定：若该 token 在旧策略下概率较高（例如 ⟦INLMATH221MATHEND⟧），即便当前概率很低，也允许较快提升；当然，前提是该 token 在采样分布中有足够可观测的质量。

7.4 采样陈旧性控制与操作含义

前面讨论的都是优化侧的裁剪，但单调提升下界还含有采样陈旧性 ⟦INLMATH222MATHEND⟧ 一项。本节处理采样侧的职责，并回到裁剪作为整体操作的准确含义。

采样陈旧性的控制

推论 5.3 表明 ⟦INLMATH223MATHEND⟧ 同样影响单调提升下界，但它无法通过优化侧的裁剪来控制，需要由采样系统实现：

  • 丢弃陈旧数据：设阈值 ⟦INLMATH224MATHEND⟧，对每个样本计算 ⟦INLMATH225MATHEND⟧，丢弃超过阈值的样本。
  • 控制策略版本窗口：限制混合采样的旧策略版本数，例如只使用最近 ⟦INLMATH226MATHEND⟧ 个版本的数据。

裁剪的操作含义

最后要澄清裁剪与理论下界的关系。

推论 5.3 里，⟦INLMATH227MATHEND⟧ 的系数 ⟦INLMATH228MATHEND⟧ 依赖于新策略 ⟦INLMATH229MATHEND⟧，所以惩罚项不能简单地换成常数。正确的操作含义是：

在 ⟦INLMATH230MATHEND⟧ 的约束下，最大化代理目标 ⟦INLMATH231MATHEND⟧

裁剪目标函数可以看作这一约束优化的近似实现——通过裁剪近似限制更新幅度，让 ⟦INLMATH232MATHEND⟧ 保持可控；在此基础上再通过梯度上升提升代理目标，尽量贴近前述单调改进分析。

本节小结

本节建立了裁剪机制的理论基础：

  1. 引理 7.1 把 TV 距离转化为样本层面的比值差，是连接理论与实现的桥梁；
  2. 两种约束方法：方法一（自适应裁剪中心）和方法二（固定增量裁剪）的硬约束版本都能保证 ⟦INLMATH233MATHEND⟧；实践中的 clipped surrogate 是对这一思路的近似实现；
  3. 与标准 PPO 对比：沿用单源 trust-region 直觉，标准 PPO 的裁剪最自然地围绕”新策略相对行为策略的偏移”施加优化压力；方法一 / 二则把压力改为围绕当前策略 ⟦INLMATH234MATHEND⟧ 控制更新增量，由此绕开多来源样本带来的结构性困难；
  4. 方法选择：陈旧性高或 LLM 大词表场景推荐方法一；若更希望”裁剪中心不再依赖旧策略族”，可选方法二（但仍需数据侧提供行为 logprob 来计算 ⟦INLMATH235MATHEND⟧）；
  5. ⟦INLMATH236MATHEND⟧ 的控制由采样侧负责，通过数据过滤和版本窗口实现；
  6. 裁剪是约束优化：在 ⟦INLMATH237MATHEND⟧ 约束下最大化代理目标。

8. 训推不一致的处理

8.1 背景与有效陈旧性

在大规模分布式训练中，推理端和训练端的策略可能不一致：

  • 数值实现差异：softmax 归一化、量化、核融合等；
  • 解码规则差异：温度缩放、top-p / top-k 采样等。

设训练侧建模的行为策略为 ⟦INLMATH238MATHEND⟧，推理端实际采样的策略为 ⟦INLMATH239MATHEND⟧。

这里讨论的是”行为策略 vs. 当前训练策略”的失配，而不是 RLHF 里常见的”当前策略 vs. reference model”的 KL 正则——后者是另一条正则化轴线。

有效陈旧性

定义有效陈旧性：

⟦DISPMATH28MATHEND⟧

这个定义同时涵盖了版本陈旧性与训推实现差异。

8.2 可操作控制

由引理 7.1，⟦INLMATH240MATHEND⟧ 可写成样本级可计算的形式。给定阈值 ⟦INLMATH241MATHEND⟧，若训练只使用满足 ⟦INLMATH242MATHEND⟧ 的样本，则被保留样本的条件分布上的有效陈旧性（记为 ⟦INLMATH243MATHEND⟧）可被控制在 ⟦INLMATH244MATHEND⟧ 以内。换言之，这里控制的是过滤后的训练分布，而不是原始采样分布上的 ⟦INLMATH245MATHEND⟧。

关键实现要点

  1. 行为分母对齐：损失中的行为概率要使用推理端记录的 ⟦INLMATH246MATHEND⟧；
  2. 概率平滑：若推理端有截断（如 top-k），需通过平滑等方式保证比值合法，并满足引理 7.1 所需的支撑覆盖条件。

9. 总结：实践指南

核心理论框架

单调提升下界的结构为：

⟦DISPMATH29MATHEND⟧

这里的 ⟦INLMATH247MATHEND⟧ 只是对前文理论常数的压缩记号，用来概括下界结构。具体来说，

⟦DISPMATH30MATHEND⟧

正文为了突出结构，把记号压缩成了 ⟦INLMATH248MATHEND⟧。它们不是实践里可以自由调节的超参数。

职责分离原则

控制项 负责方 控制手段 具体操作
⟦INLMATH249MATHEND⟧ 优化算法 策略裁剪 对更新增量进行裁剪（例如对 ⟦INLMATH250MATHEND⟧ 裁剪）
⟦INLMATH251MATHEND⟧ 采样系统 数据过滤 丢弃陈旧样本
⟦INLMATH252MATHEND⟧ 采样系统 版本窗口 仅用最近 ⟦INLMATH253MATHEND⟧ 个版本

裁剪方法选择

场景 推荐方法 理由
陈旧性较高 方法一（自适应） 自动对陈旧样本收紧约束
实现简洁优先 方法二（增量） 裁剪形式更简洁，但仍需行为 logprob / ⟦INLMATH254MATHEND⟧ 参与优势构造
LLM大词表 方法一 避免低概率token更新过慢

训推不一致的处理

  • 用推理端记录的 ⟦INLMATH255MATHEND⟧ 作为行为分母；
  • 通过样本过滤压缩参与训练数据上的有效陈旧性。

附录

A. 从状态分布差异到 average TV 的 proof sketch

证明引理 3.1 的常见起点是折扣状态访问分布的不动点方程：

⟦DISPMATH31MATHEND⟧

两式相减并整理，就可以把 ⟦INLMATH256MATHEND⟧ 写成”策略诱导转移核差异”作用在旧分布上的结果。随后对 ⟦INLMATH257MATHEND⟧ 范数取上界，利用马尔可夫核在 ⟦INLMATH258MATHEND⟧ 下的不扩张性，以及

⟦DISPMATH32MATHEND⟧

就能把状态分布差异控制到旧分布下的 average TV 上：

⟦DISPMATH33MATHEND⟧

这里省略了线性算子展开和常数整理；本文后续只用到最终的 average-TV 形式。

B. 关键符号速查表

符号 含义
⟦INLMATH259MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH260MATHEND⟧ 第 ⟦INLMATH261MATHEND⟧ 轮最新策略，第 ⟦INLMATH262MATHEND⟧ 个旧策略
⟦INLMATH263MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH264MATHEND⟧ 折扣状态访问分布，优势函数
⟦INLMATH265MATHEND⟧ 两策略在状态 ⟦INLMATH266MATHEND⟧ 上的 TV 距离
⟦INLMATH267MATHEND⟧ 第 ⟦INLMATH268MATHEND⟧ 轮混合行为策略
⟦INLMATH269MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH270MATHEND⟧ 索引转移核，索引初始分布
⟦INLMATH271MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH272MATHEND⟧ 更新增量偏移，采样陈旧性
⟦INLMATH273MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH274MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH275MATHEND⟧ 裁剪半径，陈旧性阈值，版本窗口
⟦INLMATH276MATHEND⟧ 期望优势上界系数

参考文献

  1. John Schulman, Sergey Levine, Philipp Moritz, Michael I. Jordan, Pieter Abbeel. “Trust Region Policy Optimization” (TRPO). arXiv:1502.05477. https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.05477

  2. Joshua Achiam, David Held, Aviv Tamar, Pieter Abbeel. “Constrained Policy Optimization” (CPO). arXiv:1705.10528. https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.10528

  3. John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, Oleg Klimov. “Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms” (PPO). arXiv:1707.06347. https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347

  4. James Queeney, Ioannis Ch. Paschalidis, Christos G. Cassandras. “Generalized Proximal Policy Optimization with Sample Reuse” (GePPO). arXiv:2111.00072. https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00072

  5. Yuzhen Zhou, Jiajun Li, Yusheng Su, et al. “APRIL: Active Partial Rollouts in Reinforcement Learning to Tame Long-tail Generation” (APRIL; partial rollout). arXiv:2509.18521. https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.18521

  6. Jacob Hilton, Karl Cobbe, John Schulman. “Batch size-invariance for policy optimization” (Decoupled PPO). arXiv:2110.00641. https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.00641

  7. Sham Kakade, John Langford. “Approximately Optimal Approximate Reinforcement Learning”. ICML 2002. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.5555/645531.657706

entry.summary: 本文从单策略采样的性能改进下界出发，推导 LLM 强化学习中的异策略训练问题，扩展到多策略静态/动态混合采样，并把单调提升条件拆成更新增量偏移与采样陈旧性两部分，最后落到可操作的裁剪与过滤策略。

entry.title: Choosing KL Estimators in RL: From Value Unbiasedness to Gradient Correctness

link.title: Choosing KL Estimators in RL: From Value Unbiasedness to Gradient Correctness

entry.content:
In RL, KL estimators should not be judged only by how accurately they estimate KL values, but also by what objective their gradients actually optimize. This post compares three estimators ⟦INLMATH71MATHEND⟧ in both on-policy and off-policy settings, and shows how the answer changes depending on whether KL is used as a differentiable loss term or as a detached reward-shaping term.

1. Introduction: The Role of KL Divergence in Reinforcement Learning

This post is really about one implementation-level question: when code says “add a KL penalty,” why can changing the estimator, changing the sampling distribution, or adding a single .detach() quietly change the optimization target? In policy optimization (PPO, GRPO, etc.) and alignment training (RLHF/RLAIF), a KL penalty looks like a straightforward regularizer that keeps the current policy close to a reference. But once you implement it, several choices immediately branch apart: which estimator (⟦INLMATH72MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH73MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH74MATHEND⟧), which sampling distribution (on-policy vs. off-policy), and how the KL term enters optimization (as a differentiable loss term vs. as a detached reward-shaping term).

To make that question precise, we need to separate two issues: estimating the value of a KL term, and understanding which objective its gradient actually optimizes. In many implementations those are not the same thing.

1.1 The Distinction Between Forward KL and Reverse KL

Let ⟦INLMATH75MATHEND⟧ be the current actor policy, ⟦INLMATH76MATHEND⟧ the reference policy. The two directions of KL divergence are:

Reverse KL:

⟦DISPMATH1MATHEND⟧

Forward KL:

⟦DISPMATH2MATHEND⟧

Intuition:

  • Reverse KL is mode-seeking: the policy concentrates on high-probability regions of ⟦INLMATH77MATHEND⟧, possibly sacrificing diversity.
  • Forward KL is mass-covering: the policy tries to cover the support of ⟦INLMATH78MATHEND⟧.

RLHF typically uses reverse KL because we want the actor not to stray too far from the reference, rather than requiring it to cover every mode.

1.2 Three Choices That Change the Answer

It helps to think of KL implementation as three choices: who the samples come from, which KL direction you care about, and whether KL is backpropagated directly or only used as a reward coefficient. Change any one of them, and the recommended estimator may change too.

  1. Who to sample from? Do samples come from the current policy ⟦INLMATH79MATHEND⟧ (on-policy), or from a behavior policy ⟦INLMATH80MATHEND⟧ (off-policy)?
  2. What to estimate? Are we trying to estimate reverse KL ⟦INLMATH81MATHEND⟧ or forward KL ⟦INLMATH82MATHEND⟧?
  3. How to use it? Is the KL term used as a differentiable loss term, or as a detached reward-shaping term (stop-gradient)?

Scope: This post focuses on token/sample-level KL terms and their behavior inside the main policy-gradient term. I only comment briefly on learned critics, GAE, baseline normalization, and fully rigorous off-policy correction for general multi-step MDPs.

Unlike classic notes that mainly discuss KL approximation as a value-estimation problem, this post is closer to the recent LLM-RL question of gradient correctness: once the same estimator moves from a reward coefficient to a differentiable loss term, is it still optimizing the objective you think it is?

Bottom line first (only for the token/sample-level KL terms discussed here)

  • If the target is reverse KL and KL is a differentiable loss term: in the naive on-policy implementation, use ⟦INLMATH83MATHEND⟧; if you explicitly construct ⟦INLMATH84MATHEND⟧, prefer ⟦INLMATH85MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH86MATHEND⟧.
  • If KL is used as stop-gradient reward shaping: for the policy-gradient term itself, only ⟦INLMATH87MATHEND⟧ stays aligned with reverse-KL regularization.
  • In the other common configurations, the issue is often not a slightly worse value estimate, but that the gradient is optimizing a different objective.

2. Cheat Sheet (Skim-and-Go)

The three tables below condense the entire operational guidance of the post. A minimal notation primer is given first so you can read them, pick the recommended writing for your setting, and dive back into the derivations later.

Notation primer (full definitions in the next chapter):

  • ⟦INLMATH88MATHEND⟧: current policy; ⟦INLMATH89MATHEND⟧: reference policy; ⟦INLMATH90MATHEND⟧: sampling policy (on-policy means ⟦INLMATH91MATHEND⟧).
  • ⟦INLMATH92MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH93MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH94MATHEND⟧.
  • ⟦INLMATH95MATHEND⟧: unified importance weight; ⟦INLMATH96MATHEND⟧ is stop-gradient.

2.1 On-Policy Reverse KL as a Loss Term

Loss Writing Pros Cons Rec.
⟦INLMATH97MATHEND⟧ — Gradient expectation is zero, completely ineffective ✗✗
⟦INLMATH98MATHEND⟧ Correct gradient (reverse KL), low variance, simplest implementation Value biased (but bias is minimal) ✓✓
⟦INLMATH99MATHEND⟧ — Gradient corresponds to forward KL, wrong direction ✗✗
⟦INLMATH100MATHEND⟧ Correct gradient (reverse KL), low variance, value unbiased Requires explicit ⟦INLMATH101MATHEND⟧ construction, slightly complex ✓

Note: ⟦INLMATH102MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH103MATHEND⟧ have identical gradients (sample-level equivalent). For on-policy, directly using ⟦INLMATH104MATHEND⟧ is recommended as the simplest approach.

2.2 Off-Policy Reverse KL as a Loss Term

Loss Writing Pros Cons Rec.
⟦INLMATH105MATHEND⟧ Correct gradient (reverse KL), value unbiased Higher variance △
⟦INLMATH106MATHEND⟧ — Gradient matches a local second-order surrogate, not reverse KL ✗✗
⟦INLMATH107MATHEND⟧ Correct gradient (reverse KL), low variance Value biased (but bias is minimal) ✓✓
⟦INLMATH108MATHEND⟧ Correct gradient (reverse KL), low variance, value unbiased — ✓✓

Note: ⟦INLMATH109MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH110MATHEND⟧ have identical gradients (sample-level equivalent). Both are recommended choices.

2.3 KL as Reward Shaping (stop-gradient)

Estimator Pros Cons Rec.
⟦INLMATH111MATHEND⟧ Value unbiased, policy-gradient term unbiased Higher variance ✓✓
⟦INLMATH112MATHEND⟧ Value biased Policy-gradient term biased ✗✗
⟦INLMATH113MATHEND⟧ Value unbiased, low variance Policy-gradient term biased, bias term is ⟦INLMATH114MATHEND⟧, may cause training collapse ✗✗

Note: For the stop-gradient reward-shaping setup analyzed here, only ⟦INLMATH115MATHEND⟧ keeps the policy-gradient term aligned with reverse-KL regularization. Both ⟦INLMATH116MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH117MATHEND⟧ introduce bias in that term; ⟦INLMATH118MATHEND⟧ is especially treacherous because it looks attractive at the value-estimation level yet can still destabilize training.

Legend: ✓✓ strongly recommended; ✓ recommended (slightly more complex or minor drawback); △ usable with caution; ✗✗ does not match the objective discussed here.

For derivations, variance analysis, and common pitfalls, keep reading.

3. Preliminaries: Notation and Basic Concepts

Before getting into the analysis, let’s fix the notation and write down two basic results that will be used throughout.

3.1 Notation, Sampling Distribution, and True Gradients

Notation:

  • ⟦INLMATH119MATHEND⟧: Current actor policy (parameterized by ⟦INLMATH120MATHEND⟧)
  • ⟦INLMATH121MATHEND⟧: When unambiguous, we write ⟦INLMATH122MATHEND⟧
  • ⟦INLMATH123MATHEND⟧: Reference policy (independent of ⟦INLMATH124MATHEND⟧)
  • ⟦INLMATH125MATHEND⟧: Behavior policy for off-policy sampling (independent of ⟦INLMATH126MATHEND⟧)
  • ⟦INLMATH127MATHEND⟧: Score function
  • ⟦INLMATH128MATHEND⟧: Stop-gradient operation (.detach() in code)

A Unified Perspective on Sampling Policies: Introducing the ⟦INLMATH129MATHEND⟧ Notation

When analyzing KL-estimator gradients, on-policy and off-policy may look like separate cases, but they can be handled in one framework.

Introduce the sampling policy ⟦INLMATH130MATHEND⟧, meaning data are drawn from ⟦INLMATH131MATHEND⟧. Define the unified ratio:

⟦DISPMATH3MATHEND⟧

The key insight is: in both on-policy and off-policy analyses, we treat the sampling policy ⟦INLMATH132MATHEND⟧ as a gradient constant (i.e., apply stop-gradient to ⟦INLMATH133MATHEND⟧).

  • Off-policy (⟦INLMATH134MATHEND⟧): ⟦INLMATH135MATHEND⟧ is inherently independent of ⟦INLMATH136MATHEND⟧, so ⟦INLMATH137MATHEND⟧, giving ⟦INLMATH138MATHEND⟧
  • On-policy (⟦INLMATH139MATHEND⟧): Set ⟦INLMATH140MATHEND⟧ but stop its gradient, so ⟦INLMATH141MATHEND⟧ (numerically always 1), while still having ⟦INLMATH142MATHEND⟧

In the on-policy case, even though ⟦INLMATH143MATHEND⟧ numerically, you must explicitly construct ⟦INLMATH144MATHEND⟧ (or equivalently ⟦INLMATH145MATHEND⟧) in the computation graph. If you replace it with the literal constant 1, you cut off the score-function path, causing the derivation to degenerate to the “naive on-policy implementation” described later.

What ⟦INLMATH146MATHEND⟧ restores is the gradient path coming from the sampling distribution’s dependence on ⟦INLMATH147MATHEND⟧. In the on-policy case, that missing path is exactly why expect-then-differentiate and differentiate-then-expect can disagree.

With this notation in place, the rest of the derivation no longer needs two separate tracks.

Score Function and True KL Gradients

The score function has an important property: ⟦INLMATH148MATHEND⟧ (since ⟦INLMATH149MATHEND⟧).

Using this property, we can derive the true gradients of forward and reverse KL divergences with respect to ⟦INLMATH150MATHEND⟧.

Reverse KL Gradient:

⟦DISPMATH4MATHEND⟧

Differentiating with respect to ⟦INLMATH151MATHEND⟧ (using the product rule):

⟦DISPMATH5MATHEND⟧

Using ⟦INLMATH152MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH153MATHEND⟧, and ⟦INLMATH154MATHEND⟧:

⟦DISPMATH6MATHEND⟧

Thus:

⟦DISPMATH7MATHEND⟧

Preview: We will later define ⟦INLMATH155MATHEND⟧, so the above can be written concisely as ⟦INLMATH156MATHEND⟧ — this form appears repeatedly in gradient analysis.

Forward KL Gradient:

⟦DISPMATH8MATHEND⟧

Since ⟦INLMATH157MATHEND⟧ is independent of ⟦INLMATH158MATHEND⟧:

⟦DISPMATH9MATHEND⟧

To estimate this using samples from ⟦INLMATH159MATHEND⟧, apply importance sampling:

⟦DISPMATH10MATHEND⟧

Using ⟦INLMATH160MATHEND⟧, this can be rewritten as:

⟦DISPMATH11MATHEND⟧

Preview: We will later derive ⟦INLMATH161MATHEND⟧, so ⟦INLMATH162MATHEND⟧ (forward KL). This is why directly backpropagating through ⟦INLMATH163MATHEND⟧ produces the “wrong” gradient direction when you intend reverse KL.

With these two results, we can later determine which KL’s true gradient each estimator’s gradient expectation corresponds to.

4. Three Estimators: Definitions and Design Principles

Let ⟦INLMATH164MATHEND⟧ denote the ratio. In a classic note, John Schulman compared three single-sample KL estimators that keep showing up in RLHF and LLM-RL implementations.

4.1 The Three Estimators: Definitions and Intuition

⟦INLMATH165MATHEND⟧: The Naive Log-Ratio Estimator

⟦DISPMATH12MATHEND⟧

This is the most direct definition: the negative log-ratio. It is unbiased for reverse KL, but the main issue is not that it targets the wrong thing. The issue is that a single sample can be positive or negative. Even when the true KL is small, samples can still swing in both directions, which often leads to large relative variance.

⟦INLMATH166MATHEND⟧: The Squared Log-Ratio Estimator

⟦DISPMATH13MATHEND⟧

Design motivation: ⟦INLMATH167MATHEND⟧ can be either positive or negative; squaring yields an estimator where every sample is non-negative, and each sample measures the magnitude of mismatch between ⟦INLMATH168MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH169MATHEND⟧.

Why is the bias often small? More precisely, ⟦INLMATH170MATHEND⟧ is not reverse KL itself, but near ⟦INLMATH171MATHEND⟧ it shares the same second-order local expansion. So it is best understood as a locally useful surrogate; once you leave that small-KL neighborhood, the approximation need not stay reliable.

Technical note: why does ⟦INLMATH172MATHEND⟧ share the same second-order local behavior as reverse KL?</summary>

Under the usual regularity conditions, if ⟦INLMATH173MATHEND⟧ and we perturb by a small ⟦INLMATH174MATHEND⟧, then

⟦DISPMATH14MATHEND⟧

and

⟦DISPMATH15MATHEND⟧

where ⟦INLMATH175MATHEND⟧ is the Fisher information matrix at ⟦INLMATH176MATHEND⟧.

</details>

⟦INLMATH177MATHEND⟧: The Bregman Divergence Estimator via Control Variates

⟦DISPMATH16MATHEND⟧

Design motivation: We want an estimator that is both unbiased and low variance. A standard approach is to add a control variate to ⟦INLMATH178MATHEND⟧—a term with zero expectation that (ideally) is negatively correlated with ⟦INLMATH179MATHEND⟧.

Note that ⟦INLMATH180MATHEND⟧, so for any ⟦INLMATH181MATHEND⟧,

⟦DISPMATH17MATHEND⟧

remains an unbiased estimator.

Why choose ⟦INLMATH182MATHEND⟧? Since ⟦INLMATH183MATHEND⟧ is concave, we have ⟦INLMATH184MATHEND⟧, therefore

⟦DISPMATH18MATHEND⟧

It is always non-negative. This ensures every sample contributes “positively” to the estimate, eliminating the cancellation problem of ⟦INLMATH185MATHEND⟧.

Geometric intuition: ⟦INLMATH186MATHEND⟧ is actually a Bregman divergence. Consider the convex function ⟦INLMATH187MATHEND⟧, whose tangent at ⟦INLMATH188MATHEND⟧ is ⟦INLMATH189MATHEND⟧. The Bregman divergence is defined as the difference between the function value and the tangent value:

⟦DISPMATH19MATHEND⟧

Since a convex function always lies above its tangent, this gap is naturally non-negative. More importantly, as ⟦INLMATH190MATHEND⟧, the gap shrinks at a second-order rate ⟦INLMATH191MATHEND⟧, which is the fundamental reason why ⟦INLMATH192MATHEND⟧ tends to have lower variance when the policies are close.

The design logic is easiest to see side by side:

Estimator Definition Design Principle
⟦INLMATH193MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH194MATHEND⟧ Naive definition
⟦INLMATH195MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH196MATHEND⟧ Local second-order KL surrogate
⟦INLMATH197MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH198MATHEND⟧ Control variate + Bregman divergence

5. Value Estimation: Bias and Variance

Assume we sample from ⟦INLMATH199MATHEND⟧ to estimate reverse KL ⟦INLMATH200MATHEND⟧:

5.1 Unbiasedness Analysis

⟦DISPMATH20MATHEND⟧

For reverse KL values, ⟦INLMATH201MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH202MATHEND⟧ are unbiased estimators, while ⟦INLMATH203MATHEND⟧ is biased.

5.2 Variance Characteristics

John Schulman’s experiments (⟦INLMATH204MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH205MATHEND⟧, true KL = 0.005) show:

Estimator bias/true stdev/true
⟦INLMATH206MATHEND⟧ 0 20
⟦INLMATH207MATHEND⟧ 0.002 1.42
⟦INLMATH208MATHEND⟧ 0 1.42

When KL is large (⟦INLMATH209MATHEND⟧, true KL = 0.5):

Estimator bias/true stdev/true
⟦INLMATH210MATHEND⟧ 0 2
⟦INLMATH211MATHEND⟧ 0.25 1.73
⟦INLMATH212MATHEND⟧ 0 1.7

Intuitively:

  • ⟦INLMATH213MATHEND⟧ has a first-order term; when ⟦INLMATH214MATHEND⟧ is close to 1 it can fluctuate substantially and can be negative.
  • ⟦INLMATH215MATHEND⟧ is second-order around ⟦INLMATH216MATHEND⟧ and is always non-negative, which typically yields lower variance when the policies are close.
  • Once you leave the small-KL, well-covered regime and ⟦INLMATH217MATHEND⟧ can become very large, the variance of ⟦INLMATH218MATHEND⟧ can also blow up. At that point the comparison between ⟦INLMATH219MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH220MATHEND⟧ is no longer one-sided.

If you only care about KL values, this is the quick picture:

Estimator Bias for value Variance characteristics
⟦INLMATH221MATHEND⟧ Unbiased High (can be +/-)
⟦INLMATH222MATHEND⟧ Biased (often small near KL=0) Low (always positive)
⟦INLMATH223MATHEND⟧ Unbiased Low (always positive)

So at the pure value-estimation level, ⟦INLMATH224MATHEND⟧ is often the safest choice in the common small-KL, well-covered regime.

Note: To estimate the forward KL value ⟦INLMATH225MATHEND⟧, but only sample from ⟦INLMATH226MATHEND⟧, use importance sampling ⟦INLMATH227MATHEND⟧.

6. Two Ways to Use a KL Penalty

The next fork in the road is simply how the KL penalty is used in code. That choice determines whether value-estimation properties are enough, or whether gradient properties become the real issue.

Recall the objective for KL-regularized reinforcement learning (where ⟦INLMATH228MATHEND⟧ denotes the trajectory distribution induced by policy ⟦INLMATH229MATHEND⟧):

⟦DISPMATH21MATHEND⟧

This mathematical form looks unified, but in actor-critic algorithms (e.g., PPO) it gives rise to two fundamentally different implementation paradigms. They often differ by only a few lines of code, yet correspond to different optimization semantics.

Notation: In this section, we use ⟦INLMATH230MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH231MATHEND⟧ to generically refer to a token/state-level KL estimator (such as ⟦INLMATH232MATHEND⟧), with specific definitions from the earlier section “Three Estimators: Definitions and Design Principles”.

6.1 As a Loss Term: KL Backpropagates Directly

actor_loss = -advantage * log_prob + beta * kl  # kl participates in gradient

The critic learns only the environment value function; the KL term acts as an explicit regularizer for the actor and participates directly in backpropagation.

6.2 As a Reward-Shaping Term: KL Changes Reward but Does Not Backpropagate

kl = compute_kl(log_prob_q, log_prob_p).detach()
shaped_reward = reward - beta * kl

KL is treated as part of the reward via reward shaping, and the actor-critic update is performed on the shaped reward. The KL term itself is detached and does not backpropagate.

In many implementations, these two forms differ by just a .detach(). But optimization-wise they are not the same algorithm. The basic split is:

  • KL as a loss term: Requires correct gradients for the KL component, including which objective those gradients correspond to.
  • KL as a reward-shaping term: Requires accurate KL values, and also requires that the induced policy-gradient update matches the intended objective.

7. Gradient Analysis for Differentiable KL Losses

When KL serves as a differentiable loss term, the key question is which objective each estimator actually optimizes through its gradient. This is subtle but central in practice.

We will keep using the same unified framework, so on-policy and off-policy can be handled in one derivation. Recall the ratio

⟦DISPMATH22MATHEND⟧

where ⟦INLMATH233MATHEND⟧ is the sampling policy. Within this framework:

  • On-policy (⟦INLMATH234MATHEND⟧): ⟦INLMATH235MATHEND⟧, but ⟦INLMATH236MATHEND⟧
  • Off-policy (⟦INLMATH237MATHEND⟧): ⟦INLMATH238MATHEND⟧, and ⟦INLMATH239MATHEND⟧

7.1 Basic Gradients of the Three Estimators

For an in-depth analysis of off-policy scenarios in large language models, refer to my previous blog post: From Two-Policy to Three-Policy: TRPO Extension Under Behavior-Reference Mismatch in LLM RL.

Empirical evidence: Shah et al. (2025) report that in on-policy RL fine-tuning of LLMs:

Estimator Value unbiased? Policy-gradient term unbiased under stop-grad reward shaping? Actual performance

Stepping back, value unbiasedness and gradient correctness are two separate axes. For the stop-gradient reward-shaping setup discussed here, only ⟦INLMATH461MATHEND⟧ gives the correct policy-gradient term for reverse-KL regularization. Even though ⟦INLMATH462MATHEND⟧ is value-unbiased and often lower variance, using it in reward shaping introduces a biased update and can destabilize training.

Scope reminder: once you add a learned critic, GAE, baseline normalization, and other implementation details, additional bias sources appear. The conclusion here is intentionally about the policy-gradient term itself.

At this point, an apparent tension may arise:

  • In reward shaping we emphasize “only use ⟦INLMATH463MATHEND⟧”;
  • But in the earlier loss-term backpropagation discussion (especially off-policy), we recommend using ⟦INLMATH464MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH465MATHEND⟧ for lower-variance reverse-KL gradients.

Let me explain why these are not contradictory: for the KL regularization term’s contribution to the policy-gradient update, the two implementations can be sample-wise equivalent. The practical differences arise mainly from whether the KL term enters the advantage/baseline and from the resulting credit-assignment pathway.

8.3 ⟦INLMATH466MATHEND⟧ in Reward Shaping vs. Low-Variance KL Losses

At this point the natural question is: in what sense is “KL in loss” equivalent to “KL in reward”, and in what sense is it not?

Sample-Level Equivalence of the KL Gradient Term

The equivalence discussed here is only about the gradient random variable coming from the KL regularization term itself. Once you add learned critics, baselines, GAE, or batch centering, the overall update semantics split again. We write everything in the ascent direction ⟦INLMATH467MATHEND⟧ (if you minimize a loss in code, that is just a global sign flip), and keep the same unified notation: samples come from ⟦INLMATH468MATHEND⟧, and the importance weight ⟦INLMATH469MATHEND⟧ multiplies the policy-gradient estimator.

KL as a loss term (low-variance choice): We proved earlier that when using ⟦INLMATH470MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH471MATHEND⟧ as the regularization term, the gradient random variable simplifies to

⟦DISPMATH63MATHEND⟧

KL as reward shaping (⟦INLMATH472MATHEND⟧ in reward): The shaped reward is ⟦INLMATH473MATHEND⟧ (applying stop-gradient to ⟦INLMATH474MATHEND⟧ just avoids direct KL backpropagation in implementation; it does not change the numerical penalty itself). In the policy-gradient term, the KL contribution is

⟦DISPMATH64MATHEND⟧

This is why sections 8.1 / 8.2 above and chapter 7 (loss-term analysis) are not actually in conflict: at this level, the KL gradient terms from both approaches are identical sample by sample.

In other words, ignoring the specific construction details of baseline/advantage:

  • “Writing KL into loss with low-variance implementation (⟦INLMATH475MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH476MATHEND⟧)”
  • and “Writing KL into reward with ⟦INLMATH477MATHEND⟧ (stop-gradient shaped reward)”

can exert exactly the same KL regularization “force” on policy updates.

Specifically, if we only look at the gradient term contributed by KL penalty when “maximizing ⟦INLMATH478MATHEND⟧” (the penalty term carries a negative sign in ⟦INLMATH479MATHEND⟧, so the ascent direction naturally carries ⟦INLMATH480MATHEND⟧):

  • Loss implementation (low-variance form): ⟦INLMATH481MATHEND⟧
  • Reward-shaping implementation (⟦INLMATH482MATHEND⟧ in reward): ⟦INLMATH483MATHEND⟧

They are the same random variable, not merely equal in expectation.

Where the Two Implementations Still Differ

Although the KL gradient terms are sample-level equivalent, the overall update semantics of the two approaches still differ. The differences mainly manifest in the following aspects:

1. Whether KL Enters Advantage/Baseline

KL as a loss term (equivalent to maximizing ⟦INLMATH484MATHEND⟧, but implementing the KL term as an independent, controllable force):

⟦DISPMATH65MATHEND⟧

KL is an independent regularization term, completely decoupled from advantage. The magnitude of the KL gradient depends only on ⟦INLMATH485MATHEND⟧ itself, unaffected by critic quality or baseline choice.

KL as reward shaping:

⟦DISPMATH66MATHEND⟧

KL enters advantage computation through shaped reward and gets processed by the baseline. This means:

  • KL’s influence is modulated by how advantage is constructed
  • If using a value function baseline, KL’s influence is partially absorbed

From an implementation perspective, the difference can be understood as: the Loss approach estimates “environment return” and “KL regularization” separately; the Reward approach treats KL as part of the return, so it follows all the processing you do to returns (baseline, normalization, clipping, etc.).

2. Credit Assignment: Explicit Regularization vs. Shaped-Reward Coupling

KL as a loss term: Each token/state KL gradient is local, directly affecting the update at that position.

KL as reward shaping: The KL penalty is folded into return/advantage computation and can influence earlier decisions depending on how returns are propagated.

3. Reward-Centered KL: Impact on Gradient Unbiasedness

In LLM RL (such as GRPO, PPO for LLM), a common advantage computation is ⟦INLMATH486MATHEND⟧. When KL is used as reward shaping, whether to include KL in the mean affects gradient unbiasedness.

Let samples be ⟦INLMATH487MATHEND⟧, denote ⟦INLMATH488MATHEND⟧, and use ⟦INLMATH489MATHEND⟧ for the KL penalty scalar of the ⟦INLMATH490MATHEND⟧-th sample, ⟦INLMATH491MATHEND⟧.

No centering (⟦INLMATH492MATHEND⟧): The expected KL gradient term is

⟦DISPMATH67MATHEND⟧

This is an unbiased gradient of ⟦INLMATH493MATHEND⟧.

Same-batch mean centering (⟦INLMATH494MATHEND⟧, including self): Since ⟦INLMATH495MATHEND⟧ depends on all samples (including ⟦INLMATH496MATHEND⟧ itself), the expected gradient becomes

⟦DISPMATH68MATHEND⟧

The KL regularization gradient is shrunk by ⟦INLMATH497MATHEND⟧, equivalent to a smaller effective ⟦INLMATH498MATHEND⟧. This is not strictly unbiased.

Leave-one-out centering (⟦INLMATH499MATHEND⟧): If we use ⟦INLMATH500MATHEND⟧ instead, then ⟦INLMATH501MATHEND⟧ is independent of ⟦INLMATH502MATHEND⟧, giving ⟦INLMATH503MATHEND⟧, therefore

⟦DISPMATH69MATHEND⟧

This remains an unbiased gradient, while enjoying variance reduction from centering.

Conclusion: Same-batch mean centering induces an ⟦INLMATH504MATHEND⟧ shrinkage of the KL gradient term (equivalently, a slight reduction in the effective ⟦INLMATH505MATHEND⟧). This is typically negligible for large group sizes (e.g., GRPO); for strict unbiasedness while retaining variance reduction, use a leave-one-out mean.

When Should You Choose Which Approach?

Dimension KL as a loss term KL as reward shaping
KL gradient form ⟦INLMATH506MATHEND⟧ (low-var choice) ⟦INLMATH507MATHEND⟧
Coupling w/ Advantage Fully decoupled Coupled through shaped reward
KL centering None (absolute penalty) Yes (⟦INLMATH508MATHEND⟧)
Credit assignment Local, per-token May have temporal backprop (impl-dependent)
Suitable for Want KL as an explicit regularizer Want KL to flow through shaped reward / advantage

Practical recommendations:

  1. If you want KL to stay as an explicit regularizer — separate from advantage construction and less entangled with critic quality — choose KL as a loss term, using ⟦INLMATH509MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH510MATHEND⟧.
